claim: Incidence of heart failure decreased by 10% in women since 1979.

result.claim: Incidence of heart failure decreased by 10% in women since 1979.
result.reasoning: A population-based cohort study (Paper ID: 19675911) investigating trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population between 1979 and 2000 found that the incidence of heart failure "did not change over time among men or women." This directly contradicts the claim that the incidence of heart failure decreased by 10% in women since 1979. While the study noted an overall improvement in survival after heart failure diagnosis, it explicitly stated no change in incidence for women.

evidence.text: Diabetic women had a significantly higher risk than men for cardiovascular events (RR, 1.98; 95% CI, 1.60 to 2.44) compared to nondiabetic counterparts.
evidence.source: Impact of diabetes on long-term prognosis in patients with unstable angina and non-Q-wave myocardial infarction: results of the OASIS (Organization to Assess Strategies for Ischemic Syndromes) Registry.

evidence.text: The 95% confidence interval for the incidence of heart failure among women was 277-300.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: The incidence of heart failure was 289/100 000 persons among women.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: During 21 046 person-years of follow-up, 199 women developed dementia.
evidence.source: Reproductive period and risk of dementia in postmenopausal women.

evidence.text: The relative risk of death after heart failure diagnosis was 1.33 for men.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: The mortality rate was 1.4 per 10(5) woman-years in 1991.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: The prevalence of low fitness was higher in adult females (16.2%) than in males (11.8%).
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: Women with natural menopause and more reproductive years had an increased risk of dementia.
evidence.source: Reproductive period and risk of dementia in postmenopausal women.

evidence.text: In developed countries, the prevalence was estimated to decrease from 1.6% to 0.9%.
evidence.source: Estimates of global prevalence of childhood underweight in 1990 and 2015.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer has fallen by over 25% since 1992.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: 95.4% of the patients were postmenopausal.
evidence.source: Association between CYP2D6 polymorphisms and outcomes among women with early stage breast cancer treated with tamoxifen.

evidence.text: 1930 deaths occurred among women.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: The number of women dying from cervical cancer in 1997 was 7% lower than in 1996.
evidence.source: Effect of screening on cervical cancer mortality in England and Wales: analysis of trends with an age period cohort model.

evidence.text: For women, taking the Polymeal daily represented a decrease in life expectancy with cardiovascular disease of 3.3 years.
evidence.source: The Polymeal: a more natural, safer, and probably tastier (than the Polypill) strategy to reduce cardiovascular disease by more than 75%.

evidence.text: The incidence of heart failure was 378/100 000 persons among men.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: The prevalence of low fitness was similar in adolescent females (34.4%) and males (32.9%).
evidence.source: Prevalence and cardiovascular disease correlates of low cardiorespiratory fitness in adolescents and adults.

evidence.text: For women, taking the Polymeal daily represented an increase in life expectancy free from cardiovascular disease of 8.1 years.
evidence.source: The Polymeal: a more natural, safer, and probably tastier (than the Polypill) strategy to reduce cardiovascular disease by more than 75%.

evidence.text: The 95% confidence interval for the incidence of heart failure among men was 361-395.
evidence.source: Trends in heart failure incidence and survival in a community-based population.

evidence.text: The mortality rate in the early 1960s was 6.6 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: From 1990 to 2015, reductions in risk-deleted DALY rates drove declines.
evidence.source: Global, regional, and national comparative risk assessment of 79 behavioural, environmental and occupational, and metabolic risks or clusters of risks, 1990–2015: a systematic analysis for the Global Burden of Disease Study 2015
